explicit_text: I was inside a hangar when I first heard that one of the World Trade Center towers had been struck. We gathered around a large screen TV and watched live as the second plane hit the other tower. It was clear to all of us that this was no accident but a terrorist attack. This happened early on September 11, 2001, and it set the tone for what was about to unfold in Washington, D.C.

This happened early on September 11, 2001, and it set the tone for what was about to unfold in Washington, D.C.

explicit_text: When the World Trade Center was hit in New York City, we immediately thought that Washington, D.C. might be next. The attacks in New York City were the first sign that something major was happening on September 11, 2001. It was a pivotal moment that made us prepare for possible emergencies here in the capital.

explicit_text: After the attacks in New York City, we were on high alert in Washington, D.C. We prepared our helicopters and responded quickly when the Pentagon was hit. The city was chaotic but controlled, with many agencies working together. We even took over air traffic control temporarily when Reagan Tower had to evacuate due to smoke.

explicit_text: I arrived at the Pentagon within about ten minutes after it was struck on September 11, 2001. The scene was smoky and chaotic but controlled, with people evacuating and emergency responders rushing in. We landed about 150 yards from the impact site and helped evacuate severely burned patients. The damage was initially hard to see but worsened as walls started caving in.

explicit_text: Reagan National Airport played a critical role on September 11, 2001. We received a crash alert from Reagan Tower about a possible plane going down near the Pentagon. Later, Reagan Tower had to evacuate because smoke from the Pentagon was affecting their air system, and they transferred air traffic control responsibilities to our helicopter unit. Reagan Tower also provided vital updates on unauthorized aircraft in the area.

explicit_text: Eagle One was the first helicopter to launch and arrive at the Pentagon on September 11, 2001. It stayed airborne to control the airspace and coordinate the response, including calling in other aviation units. Eagle One also took over air traffic control when Reagan Tower evacuated and later repositioned to a safer location near the Pentagon. The crew consisted of one pilot and two rescue technicians.

explicit_text: I was aboard Eagle Two on September 11, 2001, which was the second helicopter to respond to the Pentagon attack. Our crew included two pilots, myself and the unit commander, plus two medical personnel from the Uniformed Services University of the Health Sciences. We landed near the Pentagon and helped evacuate severely burned patients to the Washington Hospital Center. Eagle Two also returned to the area later to assist with surveillance missions for the FBI and Department of Defense.

explicit_text: During the September 11 attacks, we were informed by Reagan Tower that an unauthorized aircraft was heading toward Washington, D.C., which we later learned was the plane that went down near Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania. This information helped us understand the broader scope of the attacks and the restricted airspace situation. Pittsburgh became part of the unfolding story as the crash site of the fourth hijacked plane.
